source: CIS IBM CLOUD FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V1.1.0
title: Ensure no VPC security groups allow ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 to port 22

input_row.source: CIS IBM CLOUD FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V1.1.0
input_row.title: Ensure no VPC security groups allow ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 to port 22
input_row.description: VPC security groups provide stateful filtering of ingress/egress network traffic to Virtual Servers. It is recommended that no security group allows unrestricted ingress access to port 22.
input_row.rationale: Removing uncontrolled connectivity to remote console services, such as SSH, reduces a server's exposure to risk. Impact: For updating an existing environment, care should be taken to ensure that administrators currently relying on an ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 have access to port 22 through another, more restrictive, security group.
input_row.audit: Perform the following tasks to determine if the account is configured as prescribed: 1. Login to the IBM Cloud Portal at https://cloud.ibm.com. 2. At the Menu icon, select VPC Infrastructure-->Security Groups . 3. For each security group, perform the following: a. Select the security group name. b. Ensure no Inbound Rule exists that has a value that includes port 22 and has a Source of 0.0.0.0/0. Note that a port range value of ALL or a port range that includes port 22, e.g. 0-1024, are inclusive of port 22.
input_row.remediation: 1. Login to the IBM Cloud Portal at https://cloud.ibm.com. 2. At the Menu icon, select VPC Infrastructure-->Security Groups . 3. For each security group, perform the following: a. Select the security group name. b. Identify the Inbound rule to be removed. c. Using the Options icon, select Delete . Default Value: Unless modified, the default security group includes a rule with protocol set to TCP , a value of 22 and a Source of 0.0.0.0/0. References: 1. https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/vpc?topic=vpc-using-acls
input_row.impact: For updating an existing environment, care should be taken to ensure that administrators currently relying on an ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 have access to port 22 through another, more restrictive, security group.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**

This control involves checking security group rules in an IBM Cloud VPC, specifically to ensure that there is no ingress traffic from 0.0.0.0/0 allowed to port 22. Given that security group configurations are standard IBM Cloud service properties, they can be queried and verified using IBM Cloud APIs and the IBM Cloud CLI. 

The audit instructions provide a clear path to the security group settings in the IBM Cloud portal, indicating that the required configuration is accessible via IBM Cloud services. Using IBM Cloud APIs or CLI to list and check the security group rules aligns with typical automated approaches for security posture management.

**PROGRAM NAME:** ibm_vpc_security_group_ingress_restriction_port_22

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**

- **IBM Cloud API Calls or IBM Cloud CLI SDK Methods:** Use the IBM Cloud CLI or API to list security groups and their rules for each VPC.
  
- **IBM Cloud CLI Commands:**
  - Use the command `ibmcloud is security-groups` to list all security groups in the account.
  - For each security group, check the ingress rules using the command `ibmcloud is security-group-rules <security_group_id>`.

- **Specific Resources to Enumerate:**
  - Enumerate all security groups within the VPC infrastructure of the IBM Cloud account.

- **Exact Configuration Properties/Settings to Validate:**
  - For each security group, verify that there is no rule allowing ingress from `0.0.0.0/0` to port `22`. This includes checking for:
    - Specific rules allowing TCP traffic on port 22 from 0.0.0.0/0.
    - Rules with port range `0-1024` or `ALL` which encompass port 22, and source 0.0.0.0/0.

- **Clear Pass/Fail Criteria:**
  - **Pass:** No security group allows ingress traffic from `0.0.0.0/0` to port `22`.
  - **Fail:** Any security group allows ingress traffic from `0.0.0.0/0` to port `22`. 

This automated check ensures that VPC security groups do not unintentionally expose servers over SSH to the entire internet, a critical aspect of maintaining secure cloud environments.
